sentence_reviews.text: Human or synthetic comparisons are converted into a learned scalar estimate of desirability.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang et al. RLHF paper, reward model trained from human comparisons; Rafailov et al. DPO paper, preference-pair setup contrasting explicit reward modeling with direct optimization.
sentence_reviews.notes: Human comparisons to scalar reward is directly supported by RLHF; synthetic comparisons are plausible in modern extensions but not directly established by these citations.

sentence_reviews.text: A tractable objective is a surrogate, so optimizing it can improve the score while leaving the intended behavior unchanged or worse. Its direct dependencies include Preference learning, Supervised learning.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Synthesized from cited node sources plus the atlas dependency context in data/atlas.json.
sentence_reviews.notes: Limitation is reviewed as a broad synthesis/tradeoff statement for the family-level node, not as a direct quotation from one source.

sentence_reviews.text: In this atlas graph, Reward modeling is modeled as depending on Preference learning, Supervised learning and feeding downstream nodes such as Preference optimization (RLHF, DPO families), Reward-model training, RLHF & RLAIF, Reflection, critique, verifier & repair loops.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: data/atlas.json canonical node and dependency graph; generated from node dependencies and downstream consumers.
sentence_reviews.notes: This is an explicit internal atlas-graph positioning claim, not an external-world necessity claim.
